_comment: Benefit Navigator decision tree. Each node is keyed by id. type 'branch' = question with options (each option.next points to another node id). type 'answer' = terminal answer card. This single tree powers Objective 3 (navigation from program/condition/benefit/question/service/geography) and Objective 4 (the Benefit Navigator tool).

start.question: Where would you like to start?
start.help: AlaraOS helps you understand and reach benefits you may already be entitled to. It does not replace your Resource Center, physician, attorney, authorized representative, or any federal agency — it helps you work with them.

by-condition.question: What kind of care is needed?

by-service.question: Which service do you want to understand?

by-question.question: Pick the question closest to yours

options.label: Does my White Card cover home health?
Can I get paid to care for a family member?
Can I have the White Card and Medicare?
Does the VA cover home health?
Las Vegas / Clark County / Southern Nevada

eeoicpa-need.question: What do you need help with?

options.label: A condition caused by my accepted condition
Getting paid to care for a family member

owcp-need.question: What do you need help with?

va-need.question: What do you need help with?

ans-eeoicpa-hh.title: Yes — the White Card generally covers home health
ans-eeoicpa-hh.answer: If you have an accepted EEOICPA condition and a physician orders home health, your White Card generally covers skilled nursing, wound care, therapy, and home health aide services for that condition at no out-of-pocket cost.
ans-eeoicpa-hh.nextStep: Bring your accepted condition(s) and your physician's order. A nurse can verify your authorization and explain what's covered.
ans-eeoicpa-hh.trust: This is general information, not a coverage determination. Your DOL Resource Center and physician confirm what applies to your claim.

ans-eeoicpa-wound.title: Yes — home wound care is generally covered
ans-eeoicpa-wound.answer: For approved EEOICPA beneficiaries, skilled home wound care ordered by a physician is generally covered at no out-of-pocket cost — including assessment, dressing changes, infection monitoring, and advanced wound therapy.
ans-eeoicpa-wound.nextStep: If the wound results from a consequential condition, it may need DOL acceptance first — a nurse can help document it.
ans-eeoicpa-wound.trust: General information only. Your Resource Center and physician confirm coverage for your accepted condition.

ans-eeoicpa-infusion.title: Home infusion therapy is generally covered
ans-eeoicpa-infusion.answer: When ordered by a physician for an accepted EEOICPA condition, home infusion / IV therapy is generally covered at no out-of-pocket cost, with a nurse managing the line and treatment in your home.
ans-eeoicpa-infusion.nextStep: Have your physician's order and accepted condition ready for verification.
ans-eeoicpa-infusion.trust: General information only — confirm specifics with your Resource Center and physician.

ans-consequential.title: A consequential condition may also be covered
ans-consequential.answer: A consequential condition is a new problem caused by your already-accepted EEOICPA condition or its treatment. Once the Department of Labor accepts it as consequential, related care — including home health — may also be covered.
covered: Home health for the accepted consequential condition
ans-consequential.nextStep: Documentation is key — a clinician can help link the new condition to your accepted one for DOL review.
ans-consequential.trust: Acceptance is determined by the Department of Labor, not by AlaraOS.

ans-paid-caregiver.title: You may be able to be paid to care for your family member
ans-paid-caregiver.answer: Under EEOICPA in-home care benefits, a family member who already provides daily care may be able to be employed and paid (as a W-2 employee) to provide that care, with RN supervision, when authorized.
ans-paid-caregiver.nextStep: Eligibility depends on the authorized level of in-home care. A nurse can walk you through requirements.
ans-paid-caregiver.trust: General information only; authorization is determined through your claim.

ans-white-card-medicare.title: Yes — you can have the White Card and Medicare
ans-white-card-medicare.answer: The White Card and Medicare operate independently. Having one does not cancel the other. For care tied to your accepted EEOICPA condition, the White Card generally pays with no out-of-pocket cost.
covered: No premiums/copays for covered EEOICPA care
ans-white-card-medicare.nextStep: Keep both — they cover different things and can work together.
ans-white-card-medicare.trust: General information only — confirm coordination details with your Resource Center.

ans-get-white-card.title: How to get a White Card (EEOICPA)
ans-get-white-card.answer: You file an EEOICPA claim (Form EE-1 as a worker, or EE-2 as a survivor) with the Department of Labor. When a condition is accepted, you receive a White Card for covered medical care. The DOL Las Vegas Resource Center can help you file.
ans-get-white-card.nextStep: Contact the DOL Resource Center to file. AlaraOS can help you understand the steps and what care follows acceptance.
ans-get-white-card.trust: AlaraOS does not file claims or determine eligibility — the Department of Labor does.

ans-nts.title: Nevada Test Site & DOE workers may qualify for EEOICPA
ans-nts.answer: Workers at the Nevada Test Site / Nevada National Security Site, the Tonopah Test Range, and Atomic Weapons Employer facilities may qualify for EEOICPA benefits — including a White Card and covered home health — depending on their work history and condition.
ans-nts.nextStep: The DOL Resource Center can review your specific facility and dates of employment.
ans-nts.trust: Eligibility is determined by the Department of Labor.

ans-survivor.title: Survivors may qualify for EEOICPA benefits
ans-survivor.answer: Eligible survivors of a worker who would have qualified under EEOICPA may file a claim (Form EE-2) and may receive compensation. A representative or the DOL Resource Center can help determine eligibility.
ans-survivor.nextStep: Gather the worker's employment and medical history; contact the Resource Center to file.
ans-survivor.trust: AlaraOS provides information; the Department of Labor determines survivor eligibility.

ans-owcp-hh.title: Yes — OWCP can cover home health when authorized
ans-owcp-hh.answer: If you are a federal or postal worker with an OWCP-accepted injury or illness, and a physician orders home health, OWCP can authorize it at no out-of-pocket cost.
ans-owcp-hh.nextStep: Your physician's order plus OWCP authorization start care. A nurse can help track the authorization.
ans-owcp-hh.trust: Coverage depends on OWCP acceptance and authorization for your claim.

ans-owcp-nursing.title: Skilled nursing & wound care under OWCP
ans-owcp-nursing.answer: OWCP can authorize skilled nursing and home wound care for an accepted federal/postal work injury when a physician orders it. Postal (USPS) workers are covered the same way as other federal employees.
ans-owcp-nursing.nextStep: Confirm your accepted conditions and obtain a physician order; AlaraOS can explain the authorization path.

ans-va-hh.title: The VA can cover home health for eligible veterans
ans-va-hh.answer: Enrolled veterans can receive home health through VA Community Care when the VA cannot readily provide it. A VA referral/authorization and a provider order are required. In Region 4 (including Nevada), the network is administered by TriWest.

source.label: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs — Community Care

ans-va-hh.nextStep: Ask your VA care team for a community-care referral; a provider can begin once authorized.
ans-va-hh.trust: Eligibility and referrals are determined by the VA.

ans-va-wound.title: Home wound care for veterans through VA Community Care
ans-va-wound.answer: Eligible veterans can receive skilled home wound care through VA Community Care when referred and authorized by the VA, delivered by an approved community provider.

source.label: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs — Community Care

ans-va-wound.nextStep: Request a community-care referral from your VA care team.
ans-va-wound.trust: Referral and eligibility are determined by the VA.

ans-home-health.title: What home health is
ans-home-health.answer: Home health is skilled, physician-ordered medical care delivered in your home — nursing, wound care, infusion, and rehabilitation. It is different from non-medical home care, and federal programs (EEOICPA, OWCP, VA) can cover it.
ans-home-health.nextStep: Tell us your program and we'll show what's covered and how to start.

ans-local.title: Federal-benefits home health in Southern Nevada
ans-local.answer: In Las Vegas, Clark County, and Southern Nevada, EEOICPA White Card holders, OWCP-covered federal/postal workers, and VA Community Care veterans can receive covered home health. Local resources include the DOL Las Vegas Resource Center and the VA Southern Nevada Healthcare System.
ans-local.nextStep: We can connect your program to local, covered care and the right Resource Center.
ans-local.trust: AlaraOS complements — it does not replace — your Resource Center, physician, or the VA.
